title: Proof of the main conjecture in Vinogradov's mean value theorem for degrees higher than three

thm:1.1.summary: The main conjecture in Vinogradov's mean value theorem: for $s\ge1$ and $n,N\ge2$, $J_{s,n}(N)\lesssim_\epsilon N^{s+\epsilon}+N^{2s-\frac{n(n+1)}{2}+\epsilon}$, where $J_{s,n}(N)$ counts solutions of the Vinogradov system. The conjectured sharp bound, open for $n\ge4$ before this paper.

thm:1.2.summary: Sharp $l^2$ decoupling for the moment curve $\Gamma_n=\{(t,t^2,\dots,t^n):t\in[0,1]\}$ at the critical exponent $p=n(n+1)$: for $B$ of radius $\delta^{-n}$, $\|E_{[0,1]}g\|_{L^{n(n+1)}(w_B)}\lesssim_\epsilon\delta^{-\epsilon}(\sum_{|J|=\delta}\|E_Jg\|_{L^{n(n+1)}(w_B)}^2)^{1/2}$. The harmonic-analysis statement that powers everything; the $n=2$ case is Bourgain–Demeter's parabola decoupling.

thm:4.1.summary: From decoupling to exponential sums: with $t_i\in(\frac{i-1}{N},\frac iN]$ and $R\gtrsim N^n$, $(\tfrac1{|B_R|}\int|\sum_i a_ie(x_1t_i+\dots+x_nt_i^n)|^pw_{B_R})^{1/p}\lesssim_\epsilon(N^\epsilon+N^{\frac12(1-\frac{n(n+1)}{p})+\epsilon})\|a_i\|_{l^2}$. Translates the moment-curve decoupling into a bound on the Vinogradov exponential sum.

cor:4.2.summary: Vinogradov bound for a discretized set: $J_{s,n}(S_X)\lesssim_\epsilon N^{s+\epsilon}+N^{2s-\frac{n(n+1)}{2}+\epsilon}$ uniformly in $S_X$. The mean-value estimate at the level of $\delta$-separated points; an $\epsilon$-removal step (Section 5) then yields thm:1.1.

thm:6.1.summary: Brascamp–Lieb / Bennett–Carbery–Tao finiteness criterion: $\sup_{g_j}\frac{\|(\prod_j|g_j\circ\pi_j|)^{1/m}\|_{L^{2n/k}}}{(\prod_j\|g_j\|_{L^2})^{1/m}}<\infty$ iff $\dim(V)\le\frac{n}{km}\sum_j\dim(\pi_j(V))$ for every subspace $V$. The external multilinear-geometry input.

prop:6.2.summary: Multilinear Kakeya for plates: for $m$ families of $R^{1/2}\times\cdots\times R$ plates spanning subspaces $V_j$, under the non-degeneracy condition the appropriate $L^{2n/k}$ intersection integral is controlled. Geometric form of thm:6.1 usable for tube/plate packings.

lem:6.3.summary: For $M_n=n!$ pairwise distinct points $t_j\in[0,1]$, the tangent-space configurations $(V_k(t_j))_{j=1}^{M_n}$ satisfy the Brascamp–Lieb non-degeneracy condition. Verifies the hypothesis of thm:6.1 along the moment curve.

prop:6.4.summary: Uniform Brascamp–Lieb constant: for $K\ge M_n$, $\sup_{(t_1,\dots,t_{M_n})\in S_K}C(V_k(t_1),\dots,V_k(t_{M_n}))<\infty$ where $S_K$ is the set of $1/K$-separated tuples. Quantitative stability needed before iterating.

thm:6.5.summary: Multilinear Kakeya for moment-curve-tangent plates: for $M_n$ families of plates with orientations $(V_k(t_P),V_k(t_P)^\perp)$, $t_P\in I_j$, the multilinear plate-intersection inequality holds. The curved-geometry multilinear estimate that the ball-inflation step rests on.

thm:6.6.summary: Ball-inflation (multilinear decoupling) inequality: for $1\le k\le n-1$, $p\ge2n$, $B$ of radius $\rho^{-(k+1)}$ covered by balls of radius $\rho^{-k}$, the averaged product of $l^2$-square-functions over the small balls is bounded by the same over the big ball, up to $\rho^{-\epsilon}$. The engine that gains at each scale — the heart of the iteration.

lem:7.1.summary: Abstract weight-transfer lemma: if $Q_1,Q_2$ on weights satisfy sublinearity/superlinearity and $Q_1(1_B)\lesssim Q_2(\eta_B)$ on balls of radius $R$, then $Q_1(w_B)\lesssim Q_2(w_B)$. Lets sharp-cutoff estimates be upgraded to weighted ones throughout.

cor:7.2.summary: Reverse Hölder for extension operators: for $q\ge p\ge1$ and $B$ of radius $\gtrsim1$, $\|E_{[0,1]}g\|_{L^q(w_B)}\lesssim\|E_{[0,1]}g\|_{L^p(w_B)}$. Local constancy of $E_{[0,1]}g$ at scale $1$.

lem:7.3.summary: Interval rescaling: for $I\subset[0,1]$ of length $\delta^\rho$ and $B$ of radius $\delta^{-n}$, $\|E_Ig\|_{L^p(w_B)}\lesssim V_p(\delta^{1-\rho})(\sum_{|J|=\delta,\,J\subset I}\|E_Jg\|_{L^p(w_B)}^2)^{1/2}$. The affine-invariance / parabolic-rescaling step for the moment curve.

thm:7.4.summary: Linear-versus-multilinear reduction: for each $K$ there is $\epsilon_p(K)\to0$ with $V_p(\delta)\le C_{K,p}\delta^{-\epsilon_p(K)}\sup_{\delta\lesssim\delta'<1}V_p(\delta',K)$. Reduces the linear decoupling constant to the multilinear (transverse) one — the same hinge as in the parabola proof.

lem:8.1.summary: Flattening: for arbitrary collections of disjoint intervals of length an integer multiple of $R^{-1}$, the product of $l^2$-square-functions is bounded by the same refined to $R^{-1}$-intervals. Lets the iteration always work at the finest scale.

lem:8.2.summary: Lower-dimensional (degree $k-1$) decoupling inside an interval: for $I=[t_0,t_0+\sigma]$, $3\le k\le n$, $\sigma^{-k+1}\lesssim R$, $\|E_I^{(n)}g\|_{L^p_\sharp(w_B)}\lesssim V_{p,k-1}(\sigma)(\sum_{|J|=R^{-1/(k-1)},\,J\subset I}\|E_J^{(n)}g\|_{L^p_\sharp(w_B)}^2)^{1/2}$. The induction-on-dimension input — uses thm:1.2 for smaller $n$.

thm:8.3.summary: $n=3$ scheme: assuming thm:1.2 for all smaller $n$, for each $W>0$ there are weights $b_I,\gamma_I$ with $\sum\gamma_I<1$, $\sum b_I\gamma_I>W$ such that the localized decoupling quantity $A_p(u,B^3,u)$ obeys a self-improving inequality on balls of radius $\delta^{-3}$. Sets up the iteration in the first nontrivial degree.

prop:8.4.summary: $n=3$, single ball-inflation step: bounds the $\delta$-scale square-function product averaged over $B^1$-balls covering a $B^2$-ball by a Hölder mix of a coarser product and a lower-degree decoupling factor, with exponent $\alpha_1(p)$ fixed by an interpolation identity.

prop:8.5.summary: $n=3$, two-level ball-inflation: iterates prop:8.4 across the $B^1\subset B^2\subset B^3$ hierarchy to relate the finest-scale product over $B^3$ to coarser ones.

prop:8.6.summary: $n=3$, $r$-fold iterate: for $u$ with $(3/2)^ru\le1$, $A_p(u,B^3,u)$ is bounded by a product of $D_p$ (decoupling) and $A_p$ (localized) factors with geometrically decaying exponents. The recursion unrolled $r$ times.

prop:8.7.summary: $n=3$, repackaged $r$-fold iterate: $A_p(u,B^3,u)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}V_p(\delta)^{1-\sum\gamma_j}D_p(1,B^3)^{1-\sum\gamma_j}\prod_jA_p(b_ju,B^3,b_ju)^{\gamma_j}$. Normalizes prop:8.6 into the form fed to the outer ($M$-fold) iteration.

thm:8.8.summary: $n=3$, $M$-fold iterate: $A_p(u,B^3,u)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}V_p(\delta)^{1-(\sum\gamma_j)^M}D_p(1,B^3)^{1-(\sum\gamma_j)^M}\prod A_p(\dots)^{\prod\gamma_{j_l}}$ for $p\ge9$. Since $\sum\gamma_j<1$, $M\to\infty$ kills the $A_p$ remainder and forces $V_p(\delta)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}$ in degree $3$.

prop:8.9.summary: $n=4$, base ball-inflation on $B^2\subset\mathbb{R}^4$: $A_p(1,B^2,1)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}D_{p/4}(1,B^2)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}A_p(2,B^2,2)^{1-\alpha_1}D_{2p/4}(1,B^2)^{\alpha_1}$. First step of the degree-4 scheme.

prop:8.10.summary: $n=4$, $B^3\subset\mathbb{R}^4$ ball-inflation: expresses $A_p(1,B^3,1)$ via $A_p(2,\cdot),A_p(3,\cdot)$ and $D_{2p/4},D_{3p/4}$ factors with exponents $\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\beta_2$.

prop:8.11.summary: $n=4$, top-level $B^4\subset\mathbb{R}^4$ ball-inflation: $A_p(1,B^4,1)$ in terms of $A_p(2,\cdot),A_p(3,\cdot),A_p(4,\cdot)$ and several $D_{jp/4}$ factors with the full $\alpha_j,\beta_j$ numerology.

prop:8.12.summary: $n=4$ self-improving inequality plus the crucial exponent identity, valid for $p$ near $20=n(n+1)$: $A_p(u,B^4,u)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}V_{p,4}(\delta)^{1-\sum\gamma_j}D_p(1,B^4)^{1-\sum\gamma_j}\prod_jA_p(b_ju,B^4,b_ju)^{\gamma_j}$. The degree-4 analogue of thm:8.3+prop:8.7.

thm:8.13.summary: $n=4$, $M$-fold iterate: $A_p(u,B^4,u)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}V_{p,4}(\delta)^{1-(\sum\gamma_j)^M}D_p(1,B^4)^{1-(\sum\gamma_j)^M}\prod A_p(\dots)^{\prod\gamma_{j_l}}$ near $p=20$. Closes degree $4$ the same way thm:8.8 closes degree $3$.

thm:8.14.summary: Arbitrary-degree $M$-fold iterate: assuming the exponent system (71), for $p$ near $n(n+1)$, $A_p(u,B^n,u)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}V_{p,n}(\delta)^{1-(\sum\gamma_j)^M}D_p(1,B^n)^{1-(\sum\gamma_j)^M}\prod A_p(\dots)^{\prod\gamma_{j_l}}$, with the crucial exponent identity. The general-$n$ self-improvement that yields thm:1.2 by induction on $n$.

thm:9.1.summary: For $n\ge3$ and $p$ near $n(n+1)$, assuming thm:1.2 for all smaller $n$: $A_p(u,B^n,u,g)\lesssim_{\epsilon,K,W}\delta^{-(1-uW)(\eta_p+\epsilon)}D_p(1,B^n,g)$ for every $g,\delta,B^n$. Letting $W\to\infty$ this gives $V_{p,n}(\delta)\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}$, i.e. thm:1.2 in degree $n$.

lem:9.2.summary: The key inequality (77) of thm:9.1 follows once $V_{p,n}(\delta)\lesssim_{\epsilon,p}\delta^{-\epsilon}$ holds for $p$ just below $p_n=n(n+1)$. Pins down what 'closing the induction' means.

thm:10.1.summary: Appendix numerology: for $\Delta<n+1$ near $n+1$, the solution $\omega_1(\Delta)$ of the recursive system ($\omega_j=(1-\alpha_j)\eta_j+\alpha_j\omega_{j+1}$, $\eta_j=(1-\beta_j)\tfrac{j+1}{j}\eta_{j-1}+\beta_j\tfrac{j+1}{j}\omega_j$, $\omega_n=0$, $\eta_1=2$) satisfies $\omega_1(\Delta)>1$. The exponent bookkeeping that makes the iteration gain rather than lose.

edges.note: The Vinogradov bound for separated sets, after an $\epsilon$-removal argument (Section 5), gives the full mean-value theorem.
Counting solutions of the Vinogradov system is the $p=n(n+1)$, integer-frequency case of the exponential-sum estimate.
The exponential-sum bound is read off from moment-curve decoupling by discretizing and applying the square-function inequality.
For $n\ge3$, decoupling is obtained by sending $W\to\infty$ in the self-improving inequality of thm:9.1; the base case $n=2$ is Bourgain–Demeter's parabola decoupling.
Identifies the inductive hypothesis $V_{p,m}\lesssim\delta^{-\epsilon}$ ($m<n$) as what is needed.
thm:9.1 is the $M\to\infty$ limit of the arbitrary-degree $M$-fold iterate.
The lower-degree decoupling steps inside the iteration consume thm:1.2 for smaller $n$.
Each iteration cycle passes through the linear-vs-multilinear reduction.
The gain at each scale is the ball-inflation (multilinear decoupling) inequality.
Closure of the iteration requires the exponent system to have solution $\omega_1(\Delta)>1$.
Intervals are flattened to the finest scale before each step.
Sub-intervals are rescaled back to $[0,1]$ between steps.
Degree-4 instance of the general-$n$ iterate.
Degree-3 instance of the general-$n$ iterate.
The $M$-fold iterate in degree 4 is built by composing the single-cycle self-improvement prop:8.12.
Single degree-4 cycle assembled from the top-level ball-inflation on $B^4$.
...and the $B^3\subset\mathbb{R}^4$ ball-inflation.
...and the base $B^2\subset\mathbb{R}^4$ ball-inflation.
Each ball-inflation step is an application of the multilinear decoupling inequality.
Same.
Same.
Lower-degree decoupling factors come from the dimension-induction lemma.
The $M$-fold iterate composes the normalized single-cycle inequality.
Repackaging of the $r$-fold unrolled recursion.
Unroll the two-level ball-inflation $r$ times.
Iterate the single ball-inflation step across $B^1\subset B^2\subset B^3$.
The single step is the multilinear decoupling inequality at $k=1,2$.
The degree-3 scheme is set up from the normalized iterate; weights $b_I,\gamma_I$ are extracted there.
Uses thm:1.2 for $n=2$ as the lower-degree input.
Lower-degree ($k=2$) decoupling factor.
The reduction relies on rescaling sub-intervals to unit scale.
Weight-transfer lemma upgrades sharp-cutoff bounds to weighted ones.
Ball-inflation is deduced from multilinear Kakeya for moment-curve-tangent plates.
The curved-plate multilinear estimate follows from the plate multilinear Kakeya inequality.
Uses the uniform Brascamp–Lieb constant over separated tuples.
Plate multilinear Kakeya is the geometric form of the Brascamp–Lieb finiteness theorem.
Uniform constant builds on the non-degeneracy of $n!$ distinct tangent spaces.
Verifies, for the moment curve, the subspace condition required by thm:6.1.
Reverse Hölder normalizes the local $L^p$ norms in the ball-inflation argument.
Local constancy used when flattening to $R^{-1}$ scale.

main_results.description: Vinogradov's mean value theorem in the conjectured sharp range, and the equivalent moment-curve decoupling theorem.

decoupling_to_vinogradov.description: Translate the harmonic-analysis decoupling estimate into the number-theoretic mean-value bound, then remove the $\epsilon$ to reach the clean statement.

multilinear_geometry.description: Brascamp–Lieb / Bennett–Carbery–Tao multilinear estimates specialized to moment-curve tangent spaces, and the ball-inflation inequality they yield.

tools_and_rescaling.description: Weight transfer, reverse Hölder / local constancy, interval rescaling, flattening, and the linear-vs-multilinear reduction.

iteration_degree_3.description: The induction-on-scales scheme worked out explicitly for the moment curve in $\mathbb{R}^3$.

iteration_degree_4.description: The same scheme in $\mathbb{R}^4$, where the multi-level ball-inflation bookkeeping first appears in full.

iteration_general_n.description: The arbitrary-degree iteration and the induction on $n$ that closes the proof of decoupling.

BD-parabola.title: The proof of the l^2 Decoupling Conjecture

BCT.title: On the multilinear restriction and Kakeya conjectures

BBFL.title: Finite bounds for Hölder-Brascamp-Lieb multilinear inequalities

Guth-Kakeya.title: A short proof of the multilinear Kakeya inequality

Wooley-EKC.title: The cubic case of the main conjecture in Vinogradov's mean value theorem

Vinogradov.title: The method of trigonometrical sums in the theory of numbers
